rationale_for_baseline_tier: An open, displaced tibial shaft fracture is a limb-threatening surgical emergency. It requires paramedic parenteral analgesia and traction splinting, cannot be safely self-transported (unsplinted movement of displaced fragments risks lacerating the tibial artery/nerve), and needs washout, IV antibiotics and operative fixation within hours to prevent osteomyelitis and neurovascular loss. The correct disposition is an immediate 000 (Priority 1) ambulance — T5. Distal neurovascular status is intact at presentation, but that is a snapshot and does not down-grade the tier.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The foot below the injury turns pale/blue, cold, numb, or the toes stop moving, or it becomes pulseless
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — tell the 000 call-taker the moment it changes
escalation_edges.rationale: A change in distal perfusion or sensation signals arterial injury or compartment syndrome — a time-critical threat to the limb requiring the fastest possible definitive care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Keep watching his foot. If it turns pale, blue, cold or numb, or he can't move his toes, tell the ambulance call-taker straight away — the blood supply may be affected.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Heavy or uncontrolled bleeding from the wound
escalation_edges.rationale: Significant haemorrhage from an open fracture needs direct pressure around (not on) the wound and immediate escalation to the 000 call-taker.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the wound bleeds heavily, press a clean cloth gently around the wound (not on the bone) and tell 000 right away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The patient becomes increasingly faint, drowsy, more pale/sweaty or collapses
escalation_edges.rationale: Progressive faintness/collapse may indicate blood loss or a vasovagal event; lay them flat, keep them warm and update 000. If they stop breathing normally, begin CPR with dispatcher guidance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he becomes very faint, drowsy or passes out, lay him flat, keep him warm and tell 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rapidly worsening pain out of proportion, a very tight/tense swollen calf, or new pins-and-needles in the foot
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — within the first hours as swelling develops
escalation_edges.rationale: These are the early features of acute compartment syndrome, a limb-threatening emergency that evolves over hours even when the initial neurovascular check is normal.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain suddenly gets much worse, the calf feels very tight and tense, or the foot develops pins-and-needles, tell 000 immediately — this can be a serious pressure build-up in the leg.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: A parent is present and able to observe the foot, apply a dressing and stay on the line with 000
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Direct the carer to perform the simple first-aid tasks (do not move the limb, cover the wound, monitor the foot) and to clear access for paramedics, while the patient stays still.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 now and ask for an ambulance — tell them it is a bad open leg fracture with bone showing through the skin. Do not move or straighten the leg. Cover the wound loosely with a clean or sterile dressing without touching the bone. Keep him still, keep him warm, and do not give him anything to eat or drink. Watch the foot below the injury — if it turns pale, cold or numb, tell the call-taker straight away. Unlock the door for the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 immediately and request an ambulance
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours or be driven to hospital privately.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge/operation summary after the washout and fixation; take this to the GP for wound checks, suture/staple removal, follow-up imaging and rehabilitation referral.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Advising self-care, GP review or urgent-care self-transport for an open displaced fracture, missing the surgical emergency and the need for paramedic splinting.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Unsplinted transport lacerates the tibial artery/nerve or evolving compartment syndrome and delayed antibiotics/washout lead to limb ischaemia, osteomyelitis and possible amputation.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising the patient to get to ED by private car rather than calling an ambulance — reaches hospital but without traction splinting or pre-hospital analgesia and risks neurovascular injury in transit (movement of the displaced open fracture can cause vascular/nerve injury or worsen contamination).
